label: Reality Drift
author: A. Jacobs
canonical_definition: Reality Drift describes a condition where systems continue functioning while gradually losing contact with the reality they were built to represent.
short_definition: When systems remain operational while their relationship to reality weakens.
core_claim: Operational continuity does not guarantee alignment with reality.

mechanisms.name: proxy_optimization
mechanisms.effect: systems optimize measurable representations instead of underlying reality

mechanisms.name: abstraction_layering
mechanisms.effect: multiple layers of interpretation weaken grounding

mechanisms.name: feedback_weakening
mechanisms.effect: reality signals no longer force correction

observable_patterns: systems appear functional despite worsening lived outcomes
outputs remain polished but less grounded
procedures become more important than outcomes

connects_to.description: Reality Drift increases as semantic fidelity weakens.
Reality Drift often begins when systems optimize proxies instead of reality.
Constraint Collapse allows drift to persist without correction.
Reality Drift contributes to environments where appearances replace grounding.
Filter Fatigue weakens the ability to detect drift.
Recursive Compression Theory describes how layered representations contribute to drift.

examples.example: Models produce coherent outputs that are poorly grounded in reality.
Organizations preserve procedure while failing real-world needs.
Narratives become optimized for engagement rather than reality tracking.
Dashboards improve while employee experience deteriorates.

signature_lines: The absence of failure is not proof of alignment.
Systems can remain functional while losing contact with reality.
search_queries: why systems keep working while getting worse
AI systems coherent but not grounded
